summary: Joe Reis argues that AI is quietly revolutionizing how non-technical people work, citing examples of everyday people creating useful tools with ChatGPT. He emphasizes that AI is currently artificially cheap due to subsidies and venture capital, making now the optimal time to experiment and rework workflows before economics normalize.
key_points: Non-technical people are successfully using AI to solve domain-specific problems
AI skeptics often base opinions on outdated experiences with earlier models
Current AI pricing is artificially subsidized and won't last
The barrier to adoption is willingness to try, not technical capability
Those who don't adapt will be replaced by those who learned to use AI tools
money_quotes: AI reluctance feels very similar to the late '90s, when people thought texting was pointless or cell phones were a fad.
A lot of AI right now is artificially cheap. Subsidies, venture capital, or aggressive pricing strategies are offsetting the cost of running these systems.
The only real barrier left isn't the tech, but whether you're willing to try.
You won't be replaced by AI. That's the wrong fear. You'll be replaced by someone who learned to skate when you were still arguing about whether the ice is real.
key_insight: The window to experiment with artificially cheap AI tools is open now, and the real competitive threat isn't AI itself but colleagues who embrace it while others hesitate.
